speaker: interviewer
text: I am Alan Ramstein. Today is August the 9th, 1996. I am conducting an interview with Morris Kozak. The interview is being conducted in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, United States. Tell me your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Morris Kozak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell that for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-Z-A-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe Kozak.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland in 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the town is Mlynow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-L-Y-N-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were born when?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1924. March-- March 3, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where is Mlynow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mlynow is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which country is that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was under Poland until 1939. In 1939, Poland was taken apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: But excuse me. When you were born, though, in 1924, this was in what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was in Poland. OK. Fine. Tell me, Morris, who are your parents and if you had brothers or sisters who they were.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have-- I had parents. My father's name was Itzak Kozak, my mother's name was Chava Kozak. And then I had a-- an older brother, Reuben Kozak, and a sister, Jean Kozak, and a sister, Karen Kozak.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And were any of your grandparents living at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I remember very, very, vaguely my-- my-- one of our grandmothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I remember-- my father's mother I remember very vaguely and I remember my mother's mother, which she-- she-- she was killed by the Germans during the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandparent's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents' name was-- my father's parents, the father was Moshe, which I'm named after and my mother was-- and-- and my grandmother was Edith. And my mother's parents, I only remember the grandmother, which her name was-- used to call her Riva, Riva [POLISH]. I forgot-- well, my grandfather I don't remember because he died very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, before the war started, where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Mlynow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was living in the family with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my brothers and sisters and my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And could you tell me what your life was like and how old you were before the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war broke out, well, life was kind of hard in those days but it was a way of life. We didn't know no better. And when the war started, I think I was 17 or 18-- 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what was your life like? Could you give me a little bit of like were you a student or were you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I went to school for seven years and at the same time we were working in the family-- we had a little farm, we had a little business. And-- and then we worked in a mill, you know, with the horses. And everybody was working. And that was a way of life for everybody, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before the war broke out, you did some work around the farm. Is that it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's true.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And could you tell me what was it like in the Jewish community, what was life like in--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- the Jewish community was tight-- tight knit community around a synagogue and everybody-- we knew who we were. And with-- and everybody knew everybody else. The main gathering was in the synagogue. We lived around a synagogue. And every evening, we used to go to evening prayers and after the prayers, they used to sit-- different people from different businesses, they'd sit and talk. There were some people sitting and learning there. The-- the-- the books, every-- and-- and that-- that was the get-together in the synagogue. You sit there for two, three hours in the evening and everybody went home. And every day was the same thing. It was a way of life and everything was tradition and that's how we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any experience before the war of any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of experiences was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jew, under Poland, we were discriminated. There were signs written all over the-- you could see [POLISH], in other words, [POLISH] in English would say depress the Jews, you know, keep them down. And Jews, the-- the Palestina, the Jews go to Palestine. And there were Jewish little stores where you had Polaks standing in front of the door that wouldn't let a Polak go into the shop from a-- from a Jew. In other words, we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: You say a Polak? What is a Polak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polaks, you know, there were organizations, anti-Semetic. They wouldn't let a Polak go into a Jewish store to shop because you have to go to your own kind. And a Jew couldn't get a job with the street-- to sweep the streets. Couldn't get a job in a street or to be a letter carrier. That's a-- that was a no-no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you personally experience any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Personally? Well, in school the-- the-- the-- there Polish kids used to call us [POLISH], you know. We were-- we were always fighting, the gentile kids and the Jewish kids, we-- we were Jews. And that's the way it was. It was a way of life. We didn't know any different.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the public school, which is run by the-- by the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what languages did you speak in school and what languages did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, we used to speak Polish. On the street, the majority of the people were Ukrainian, so you'd speak Ukraine. At home, you used to speak Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the war broke out, where were you when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out, I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm talking about 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939, I was at home. Seen the Russians march in.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how did life change, what was the change that took place when the war broke out in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I-- it was a new-- a new era, a new-- the Polish people, you know, there was no more Poland and sort of, I don't know. It's hard to tell. We didn't know the regime, we didn't know the-- what's right before us. But nobody bothered us really.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did your life change very much, or was any change in your life as a result--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not right away. But we could see it coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: They tried-- the government was organized [INAUDIBLE] these collective farms, you know, and different things, you know. Everything was starting to run by the government. Than the war came in '41 and everything was disrupted, everything was cut off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we go back just for a minute? I wanted to find out what sort of occupation and what your father did to make a living.

speaker: interviewee
text: Make a living, he were working in a mill. It was a flour mill. He were hauling wheat and flour to the railroad station. And then we had a little business from coal, lime, which was run by my father and my mother and the kids. Everybody worked. And this was a very-- we used to sell the lime and the coal to the farmers. You know, they used to come. And this was a way of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you find any change in this way of life when the war broke out, then, in terms of how your father made a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean when the Russians came?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were still doing more or less the same thing. They didn't bother us yet. I don't know what the future would hold, but-- you now, but as of that time, wasn't much change.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while the Russians were there, at what point did there start to be a change in how your family lived and how you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we still lived approximately the same way, but they started to-- like the mill became already government property. It wasn't-- they took it away from the owner. The big stuff, the big enterprises, right away became government. But we were a little, you know, so in time probably it would have changed for us, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So did life remain pretty much the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty much the same, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you notice the next change in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next change was when the Germans came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war start-- the war started in June, June the 20th, and within-- that was on a Sunday morning. And the-- and the Germans came to us I remember Thursday or Friday they were already there. It took them only four days or five days, we were occupied by the Germans. And we seen a lot of Russians running away and-- and as far as us Jews, right away, we knew we were in trouble. Right away they took away the horses, the cows, everything. But we never thought they're going to take our lives away. But we were wrong. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --within a few days, we heard they'd shot two boys. They were walking somewhere, they got killed by the Germans. The rabbi was taken away eight or 10 days later, picked up and never came back. Till today we don't know what happened to him. And a few more prominent people from the time, they disappeared. With the help of the Ukrainians, they took a big part in our lives because they knew everybody, they knew who was what and who was-- who was Jewish and who was not Jewish. And the-- they did a-- they did a good job.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say they did a good job. In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rounding us up, you know, and everything. In other words, like they were taking us to work a lot. They appointed a few Jews, they called it the Judenrat. And whenever they needed people, they just went to the Judenrat and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. What is the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judenrat means like a-- like a-- they were the-- they were-- the Judenrat consisted of let's say four or five people from the town. And if they needed something, they didn't go to each individual, they went to the Judenrat and the Judenrat had to supply them this money people for work or this many people for this, whenever they needed. That was the Judenrat. And I remember when they came in the first summer, right outside of town there was an airport which the Russians built and they bombed it when the war started. And there were a lot of holes--

speaker: interviewer
text: You say they bombed it. Who was they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. When they attacked Russia, they bombed the airport. There were a lot of holes there. And they wanted to use it so they had to fill the holes. Right not far from us, there was a big building, they call it a palace-- we used to call it a palace. And so they dynamited it and they took so many Jews. There were Germans with tractors and platforms. We were taken over after they blew it up, take all that stuff on these platforms and they-- they used to bring it to fill in the holes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were working there on the-- on the ground for the Germans. And there, a lot of incidents happened while we were there. Life didn't mean nothing no more, like-- like I was telling you. I'll tell you a little incident what happened. We had a lot of religious Jews there, they were-- had beards and they didn't want to be shaved. But they-- so they used to take a scarf and tie it around their beard.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they-- they-- they had to shave their beards. So they took a barber and they took a big rock in that place where they blew up that palace. There was a big rock there and-- and they picked out the Jews with the beards and bring it to him and he had to shave them. As the tractor drove by and he was standing against the rock, he turned the tractor with his chains against that rock. He chopped both his feet off, the barber. He started to holler. So the German, the one who was driving the tractor, took out a gun, shot him right on the spot. But-- so we knew we were in trouble, very much so. On the airport, there were different people working there and there were telephone lines and whatever running on the airport. They took and they-- some kind of line got cut. And the nearest 12 guys that were working to fill in the holes there, they took them out and they shot them, from my home town. But actually, they didn't cut the line. A tractor ran by and cut the line. But they blamed them. So life didn't mean nothing no more. We knew-- we knew-- this is-- incidents-- every day, something else happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your daily life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daily life, go to work and we-- then my father went somewhere else to work. My sisters--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you-- excuse me. How did you know where to go to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- lots of times, I used to go, I knew farmers. If they needed help, I used to go and work for nothing just to keep me away from the ghetto. But that's-- that was afterwards. After that incident--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. You mentioned the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was created.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the ghetto created?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the ghetto was created at the beginning of 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like? What sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I very little in the ghetto. I used to come and stay a few days. My father lived in the ghetto, my mother. My sisters, they were young kids. They used to take care of the cows for different farmers, you know, in pasture. That was their job. And my father later on became a-- a driver. We were horsemen, you know, we knew a lot about horses. And they-- the Judenrat, they came to the Judenrat and they asked them, can they supply somebody who knows how to really handle horses. So the Judenrat picked my father. I wasn't there. I was working on farms, different places. And they made him for the driver. He used to drive around the Germans to different villages, wherever they had to go. And that was his job. But he had the right to drive and out of the ghetto because he used to keep the horses in our stable, which was in-- inside the ghetto. And he was the messenger. Anything was going on outside, because we had very little communications with anybody, if-- he was the messenger before the holes were dug-- dug up already, which a lot of people knew. The Ukrainians, they used to tell him that they're digging holes. And he brought in the news and that's-- he was the-- the-- the-- the-- the communication man. And that's-- that's the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the ghetto was created, did your family have to go anyplace different or they stayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We stood-- our house fell into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in your family at the time? Tell me the names of your brothers and sisters. I don't know--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reuben.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But he worked outside the ghetto as a-- what do you call them, a toolmaker in a-- in a shop which was run by a Czech. He was working there. And most of time, he used to sleep there, too. He didn't want to go into the ghetto to sleep because you never know what's going to happen at night in the ghetto. And my sisters were working on the farm. And my father--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, Jean and Karen. Most of the time, they used to take care of the-- the-- the herds, you know, for the cars, you know, in the pasture. And my father was a driver and my mother was in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other sisters other than Jean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Karen. My sister, Karen. Younger one. She was only, what, about 10 years old at that time, eight, nine years. So we tried not to stay and I-- I always tried not to stay in the ghetto. I used to come and if they needed somewhere, I used to go away from the ghetto. So this was in 1942-- this was in 1941, beginning if 1942. Then I came to the-- back from that-- that place. They sent me to a big farm to work called [POLISH]. It was about seven kilometers south, I'll say, away, agricultural big farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say they sent you. Who send you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat sent me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was working. There was a lot of different Jews from different-- women, mostly young, women and-- and-- and boys from different towns, adjacent towns to us. And we worked there, different thing. They worked in the fields. My job was I used to carry the wheat. I used to thresh the wheat on the fields and the wagons used to come with gentile people driving horses. And my job was to carry the wheat up there the warehouse. The warehouse, it was our big stable like, you know, you could keep maybe the 100 horses. On top was a warehouse with a cement floor. It was thirty-some steps up and down. We used to carry the wheat all the day long up and down and we used to-- we used to clean the wheat from the different things, the different people in there. I used to work the steps, carry the bags all day long. That was my job. And at night was no picnic, either. They used to do with us whatever they wanted to do. A German came in, an SS man. He used to say, Juden, [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- you are dirty, you all need a bath. Over there was a little mill, and we used to discharge a lot of water, like in a pond. It was dirty. He used to make us all undress and go into that pond in the middle of the night, take a bath, take us out. Now, he says, you're clean. That was fun with us. They're making a lot of, you know, different things-- undescribable. There were women-- they were telling us they used to pick out the most beautiful girls, they used to cut their hair off. They cut off all their hair. In other words, they shouldn't have no-- any association with the Germans. Was-- one SS man used to take two-- two or three little-- two or three girls and he'll go up stairs on the steps and put them down at the bottom. And excuse my expression, he used to urinate on them, on the Jews. It was a way of having fun. In other words, they used to make you like you're dirt, you're nobody, you're nothing. Which we knew who we were. And I had worked there for-- for quite-- for a couple of months. Then I was brought back.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were working there, did you stay there or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't come back to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We stayed there. We were on-- on the watch all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were brought back and I was in the ghetto for a while-- not long. And they create-- they sent some people to a place called Studanka.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-U-D-A-N-K-A, Studanka. That was near a town Kremenents and [INAUDIBLE]. But There was a town, [INAUDIBLE], and a town, Kremements. This was in between. This was-- we were digging there peat moss. And the women, the girls, we used to dig with a certain shovel. It came out like a brick. And then-- then with-- with wheelbarrows, we used to spread them out all over the place and the girls used to put them to dry. The air should be able to get in, like stacks, high stacks. And they dried them. And I was working there for quite a while. They used to keep us in-- in barns, the men separate and the women separate, under the Ukrainian-- under the Ukrainian guards. One day, I decided I had enough of it. And [INAUDIBLE] after that round everybody up, they're taken back to the barns, I decided I'm not going back. I crawled into one of those stacks, inside. And I stayed there and they rounded everybody up. I-- got away with it because I made believe I was working there and I crawled in there and that-- that was the end of me. They took everybody away. I knew my way around all over there, the roads, because-- and-- and I run away from there. This was about 50 kilometers, different ways. I came back-- came back to the ghetto and I-- and-- and this was already in '42. Sure enough, there I heard later, we heard they took all these boys and girls to the town of [INAUDIBLE]-- it was a little Jewish town-- and they killed them all out. When I came back to the ghetto, the ghetto still existed, I went away to a Czech in a-- in a-- in a village called Podize, Podize.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In English. P-O-D-I-- Podi-- I-Z-E or some-- Podize. There was-- there was a Jew-- a Czech settlement there. The Czechs were very good people. They helped whenever they can. So I run away to the-- not run away. I went to-- from the ghetto, got out of the ghetto and I run away to him to work for him on a-- he had a big farm. He had different farm hands. He knew I-- who I was, I was-- I was Jewish. And he had a son in our town, too. That son was a good friend of-- with my father all these years. Anyway, he let me work. Then it started to get a little bit too hot. He said to me, I'll tell you what, I have a son in a different village about 30 kilometers away named [INAUDIBLE], also a Czech settlement. He says, you go work for my son.

speaker: interviewee
text: So sure enough, he drove me to there and I started to work for his son. It was a little town, [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], they used to call it, a little Jewish town like ours. And I was working there. One day I was plowing and--

speaker: interviewee
text: [CLEARS THROAT] --this was already late-- almost the fall. They-- they had potatoes in that field and I was plowing. And sometimes some potatoes are left in the field. I had a little Ukrainian girl picking the potatoes up-- going in back on me. If there's any potatoes, she used to pick them up. And-- [CLEARS THROAT] --I see-- and this was near the roads, crossroads-- the road goes into [POLISH]-- three trucks with Ukrainian police, probably Germans, too, I couldn't see it, singing away, driving up to there, to that Jewish town. The little girl comes over to me and says-- they called me fuhrer. You know, everybody know what the name [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the name [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: I gave myself the name [INAUDIBLE]. I couldn't call myself Moshe, so-called myself [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE], says to me, the Ukrainian, you see they're going to kill all the Jews today in [INAUDIBLE]. Come on, I'm going to get some stuff there. You don't have no shoes. You could use some shoes. I say, oh, to hell with the Jews. I don't want to go there. Sure enough, she left me on the field and she went away to [INAUDIBLE] and later in the day, she came back with two big bundles of clothes and shoes. And she was making fun of me, I'm stupid. You didn't go. You could have gotten so much stuff there. They killed off all the Jews. And that's the way it was. And later on, I was plowing a different spot--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. We're going to take a tape break right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead.
